data.title: Buyers Strike: Pending Home Sales Drop Further, Plunge In Midwest, Near Record Lows In All Regions As Supply Balloons
data.description:
And to top it off: cancellations of pending sales rose to a record rate in July.

Demand in the housing market sagged further: Pending home sales dropped by 0.4% in July from June, seasonally adjusted. They have now spent nearly three years crawling along, or setting, record lows, according to data going back to 2010 from the National Association of Realtors on Thursday (historical data in the chart via YCharts):

It’s even worse: the cancellation rate. Pending sales are based on contract signings and track deals that haven’t closed yet and could still get canceled because buyers cannot afford or even get homeowner’s insurance, or for other reasons. Signed contracts that then get cancelled are included in the pending sales here, but are not included in closed sales reported later.

Alas, Redfin reported that in July, 58,000 pending sales were canceled, amounting to 15.3% of all pending sales - the highest rate for any July in Redfin’s data going back to 2017.

Pending sales in all regions near record lows. Pending sales fell sharply in the Midwest, but also declined in the South and Northeast. The West was the only region where pending sales rose. Pending sales remained near record lows in all four regions.

Pending sales of existing homes in the US, compared to the Julys in prior years:

  • 2024: +0.7%
  • 2023: -6.5%
  • 2022: -21%
  • 2021: -36%
  • 2020: -41%
  • 2019: -32%

The buyers’ strike continues because prices are too high after shooting up by 50% or more within 2-3 years. Those prices no longer make economic sense.

While inventory surged. This plunge in demand coincides with surging inventories of existing homes for sale - despite large waves of delistings by frustrated sellers - leading to the most supply since July 2016 over the past three months, along with May 2020.

Split out by single-family homes and condos: Supply of single-family homes rose to 4.5 months, while supply of condos has been over 6 months since April, the highest since 2012 at the end of the Housing Bust. Here is the combined supply:

In the South, pending sales dipped 0.1% month-to-month, seasonally adjusted.

A map of the four census regions is posted in the comments below.

Compared to the Julys of prior years:

  • 2024: +1.8%
  • 2023: -9%
  • 2020: -39%
  • 2019: -30%

But inventories surged across the South.

In Texas, inventory of existing homes for sale in July jumped by 24% year-over-year, and by 32% from July 2019, to the highest in at least the decade of data from Realtor.com (we discussed the inventories of the big metros in Texas here).

In Florida, active listings in July jumped by 23% year-over-year, and by 29% from July 2019, according to data from Realtor.com (we discussed Florida’s by-metro inventories and days on the market here):

And, for example, in the Atlanta metropolitan area, active listings spiked by 31% year-over-year in July:

In the West, pending sales rose by 3.7% in July from June, which had been the second-lowest on record.

Compared to the Julys of prior years:

  • 2024: -1.9%
  • 2023: -6%
  • 2020: -47%
  • 2019: -40%

Active listings in the West have also been surging.

In Los Angeles County and in the San Francisco metropolitan area, active listings reached the highest level for any July in the decade of data from Realtor.com (discussion of inventories in the biggest markets of California is here).

In other markets in the West, inventories have also surged, reaching the highest for any July in the decade of data in the metros of Denver, Seattle, Salt Lake City, Phoenix, Tucson, Portland, and Las Vegas (discussion by metro here).

For example, active listings in the Denver-Aurora-Centennial metro are up by 48% from July 2019 (dotted purple), and along with June, the most in the decade of data from Realtor.com.

In the Northeast, pending sales dipped by 0.6% month-to-month, seasonally adjusted.

Compared to the Julys of prior years:

  • 2024: -0.6%
  • 2023: -2%
  • 2020: -42%
  • 2019: -31%

In the Northeast, inventories have also grown, but remain tighter than in other parts of the US.

For example, in the Boston-Cambridge-Newton metro, inventory jumped by 25% year-over-year and by 61% from July 2023, to be roughly level with July 2020 but well below the prepandemic years.

In the Midwest, pending sales dropped by 4.0% in July from June, seasonally adjusted, to the second-lowest sales in the data going back to 2010.

The record low had been July 2024, and compared to this record low, sales were 1.3% higher.

Compared to the Julys of prior years:

  • 2024: +1.3%
  • 2023: -8%
  • 2020: -39%
  • 2019: -29%

Inventories in some of the big metros in the Midwest diverge, rising sharply in some, such as in the metro of Columbus, OH, but less so in the metros of Detroit and Chicago:

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 2 BDCs That Should Navigate The Dividend Cutting Spree That Could Be Coming
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past several months I've downgraded many BDCs (BIZD) due to potential headwinds from lower base rates. And after the Fed's Jackson Hole speech, it looks evident we'll be seeing lower rates by the end of this year.

Before, uncertainty surrounded the direction of interest rates due to tariffs and persistent inflation. And while the market reacted positively to Powell's speech, the BDC sector will continue to see headwinds from lower base rates.

This is because most have predominantly floating-rate portfolios. And while many higher-quality BDCs have surpluses that help can sustain tightened dividend coverage temporarily, I still expect some BDCs to cut their dividends in the near-term.

However, there are some that I think can potentially avoid dividend cuts as a result of their high quality, spillover, track records, balance sheets, and unique advantages they carry.

In this article, I discuss two BDCs that I think could potentially avoid cutting their dividends in the not too distant future.

Rate Cuts Are Likely Happening

After the FED's speech the market rallied to new highs as confidence in seeing rate cuts shot up, now expecting rates to be below 4% by the end of the year, down from the current 4.50%. This will provide relief for businesses as well as consumers, although the impacts aren't likely to be felt until a few months after.

And while this is good news for businesses as well as consumers like you and me, lower base rates act as a double-edged sword for the BDC sector. Lower base rates also provide relief to borrowers, but due their lenders' predominantly floating-rate portfolios, net investment income will likely fall.

Yes, management teams could see an increase in activity as a result, but the question is will it be enough to offset the expected decline in their bottom lines?

In an article in early August I discussed how some BDCs were already forced to cut their dividends and how I have been downgrading most due to headwinds from lower interest rates, and that I suspected more were coming if we did see interest rates come down this year.

Even for the larger, more well-known BDCs like Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) & Ares Capital (ARCC), two of my favorites by the way, have seen their dividend coverage shrink in the past year.

While I suspect ARCC may avoid a potential cut due to their long operating history, size, scale, available liquidity, and fundamentals, BXSL seems at higher risk of a potential dividend cut in my opinion. I discussed this in earlier articles you can read here and here.

However, these two are not the ones I'll discuss today. Although, they're probably two of the most fundamentally-sound in the sector. Without further ado let's get into these two BDCs. And for the record, I'm not saying these are the only two, these are just two I think can also avoid.

#1 Fidus Investment (FDUS)

The first BDC, FDUS may be a surprise for some. Those who follow me likely know my favorite BDCs, which I discussed two, ARCC & BXSL previously. But over the past year, Fidus Investment has impressed me with how they've navigated lower base rates and macro uncertainty so far.

I covered FDUS more in-depth in an article you can read here. But their latest quarter saw their bottom line remain flat on an annual basis at $0.57 per share. But in total, NII grew from $18.4 million to $20 million. Their top line also grew, a result of higher fee income as well as investment activity.

Despite recognizing some losses during the quarter due to exited investments, NAV managed to grow $0.11 quarter-over-quarter to $19.57. And on an annual basis, NAV increased $0.07, a testament to their overall credit quality.

Losses of $7.6 million offset gains of $6.8 million. But here's why I think FDUS could potentially avoid a dividend cut. FDUS has one of the lowest-leveraged balance sheets with a debt-to-equity ratio of just 0.7x, well below the peer average of 1.19x.

Liquidity was plentiful with nearly $253 million in total liquidity, which included $91.2 million in cash. Besides $225 million due next year, they have no debt maturing until 2029. Additionally, non-accruals improved from the prior quarter. These were 2.9% at cost and 0.5% at fair value, down from 3.9% and 0.8% respectively.

But the secret weapon for FDUS' performance in my opinion has been the equity stakes they take in companies. Subsequent to quarter's end they took an equity stake in one new company and recognized a $0.4 million gain from two equity distributions. Moreover, equity investments accounted for nearly 88% of all portfolio companies.

And taking equity stakes in portfolio companies gives BDCs additional access to capital. And this income could be used to fund dividend shortfalls if necessary. Since inception in 2011, FDUS has realized nearly $313 million in equity investments.

Aside from this, FDUS had $1.39 in spillover income. And although the BDC has been paying out most of their income in dividends & supplementals, base dividend coverage was strong at 132%, giving them flexibility should they see tighter dividend coverage in the coming months.

While I suspect they will eliminate the supplemental, I believe the base dividend of $0.43 will remain safe.

#2 Capital Southwest (CSWC)

Second on the list is one of my longtime favorites, Capital Southwest. Ares Capital had long been my #1 BDC, and I preferred them over peers as a result of their size, scale, and liquidity advantages.

But CSWC's internally-managed structure and switch to monthly dividends has surpassed ARCC as my favorite at the moment. I discussed them more in-depth here. But in their latest quarter, I was impressed with their improvements after criticizing them previously.

Despite their net investment income declining $0.05 quarter-over-quarter to $0.61, this was still sufficient enough to cover the base dividend of $0.58. But like FDUS, NII actually grew modestly on a total basis from $31.1 million to $31.2 million.

Other improvements were their debt-to-equity ratio, which declined from 0.89x to 0.82x and non-accruals on a fair value, which decreased from 1.7% to 0.8%.

Last year, non-accruals were as high as 3.5%, something I told investors to remain cautious of and keep an eye on. Payment-in-kind also declined, accounting for 5.8% of TII, down from 7.6% in Q4.

NAV declined $0.11 from the previous quarter due to restricted stock issuance. But annually, this was a modest $0.01 decline to $16.59. Impressively, during the quarter, CSWC realized $27.2 million in gains from two equity investment exits.

Capital deployed into equity investments totaled $1 million across 3 new companies. Management also stated they had $33.2 million in unrealized appreciation from their equity portfolio, or $0.60 per share.

And in the last two quarters, management has realized $41.3 million in gains from equity investments. And lastly, undistributed taxable income increased from $0.79 in the previous quarter to $1 per share, a result of realized gains from their equity portfolio.

This, along with receiving approval for their second SBIC license gives CSWC plenty of available liquidity and flexibility to navigate dividend coverage challenges when interest rates are lowered in the coming months.

The company expects to start paying their monthly dividend of $0.1934 this upcoming October and declared a $0.06 supplemental, payable at the end of the year.

In total, this amounted to $0.6402, slightly higher than the $0.61 they saw in the latest quarter. However, base coverage was solid at 105%.

They also had $444 million in cash on their balance sheet and increased their credit facility by an additional $25 million and have no debt maturing until October of next year.

Also like Fidus Investment, if Capital Southwest does face tightening coverage, I expect them to eliminate the $0.06 supplemental, while keeping the base dividend intact.

Risks & Bottom Line

Although I suspect Fidus Investment & Capital Southwest could navigate potential dividend cuts, unforeseen circumstances such as a weaker than expected economy could increase risks and result in downside.

Moreover, having spillover income is not an indication of a safe dividend, although this can help with shortfalls temporarily.

But due to both BDCs focusing on equity investments, ample liquidity, and well-staggered debt maturities, this gives me confidence in their ability to navigate anticipated dividend cuts due to lower base rates.

data.title: KRE: Best August Since Inception Tracking, Expecting More Big Gains
data.description:
Small business optimism is up sharply off the April lows, according to the monthly NFIB Business Optimism Index. We'll get the August read shortly after Labor Day, but the vibes among entrepreneurs appear to be on the mend.

Couple that with a much steeper Treasury yield curve, and we could have a favorable setup for the long-underperforming regional bank industry. Bear in mind that some of the unrealized bond losses from 2023 have matured. Moreover, two and a half years removed from the US regional banking crisis, managers of small and mid-sized banks have likely had a chance to shore up their balance sheets.

Recall back in January, I pointed to a steeper Treasury yield curve as being a potentially bullish macro trend for the SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (NYSEARCA:KRE). Shares are up 5% since that analysis, and I reiterate a buy rating today.

NFIB Small Business Optimism Rising Once Again

2s10s Yield Curve Steepens Further

According to the issuer, KRE seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the total return performance of the S&P Regional Banks Select Industry Index, which is a modified equal-weighted index that provides the potential for unconcentrated industry exposure across large, mid, and small-cap stocks.

KRE is a medium-sized ETF with $3.7 billion in assets under management as of August 27, 2025. That's down materially from $4.9 billion at the time of my previous assessment. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is solid at 2.38%, more than a full percentage point above that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has been very strong since the April post-Liberation Day lows, earning the product a robust A- ETF grade in that category by Seeking Alpha's quantitative scoring system. Still, KRE is highly risky, given its portfolio of small-cap value stocks tied to expectations around the US economy.

From a liquidity point of view, the ETF is highly tradable-average daily volume is north of 13 million shares, while its 30-day median bid/ask spread is tight at only two basis points, per SSGA Funds.

For an update on the portfolio, the 1-star, Neutral-rated ETF is among the purest US small-cap value plays there is from an industry perspective. More than half of the allocation is housed in the lower-left section of the style box, and there is just 2% large-cap access.

That presents risk and opportunity: If the US economy avoids recession, then KRE has the potential to sharply outperform given the better interest rate backdrop and looser regulations. The One Big, Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) could add fuel to the bullish narrative, as US businesses big and small can now fully expense capital investments. Hence, those firms are more wont to apply for loans from some of KRE's largest component companies.

On valuation, with a price-to-earnings ratio barely above 11x, investors don't have to pay up for this ETF. Furthermore, KRE's long-term earnings growth rate is north of 12%, resulting in a compelling PEG ratio of under 1.0x.

KRE: Portfolio and Factor Profiles

KRE is, of course, a 100% financials-sector portfolio. What I want to call out there is that the fund's dividend has held up well in recent years, regional banking turmoil notwithstanding. I would not be surprised to see a record-high net distribution to shareholders this year.

With a modified-equal-weight portfolio construction, prospective investors don't have to be overly concerned about single-stock concentration risk. Of course, earnings season begins in mid-October, so we could see volatility tick up by then.

KRE: Holdings and Dividend Information

In terms of seasonal performance trends, we are in the throes of a weak stretch. Over the past 10 years, KRE has been lower, on average, in both August and September. October-November is the most bullish period, however.

KRE owners must love that 2025's August is the best August return in the fund's history.

KRE: Bucking the Usual Bearish August Trend

KRE: Best August on Record

The Technical Take

With intense bullish momentum in what's usually a bearish month, KRE's technical situation is generally positive. Notice in the chart below that shares are at fresh year-to-date highs, in the mid-$60s. The ETF broke above key resistance at the $64 mark just recently, and the late-2024 post-election peak of $70.25 is absolutely in play.

With a rising long-term 200-day moving average and following a bullish golden cross moving average pattern, the bulls control the primary trend. Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph-it ranges in a favorable zone between about 40 and 80. I'd like to see KRE rally through a notable area of volume by price, but the all-time high of $78.81 is still a ways off.

Long-term support is seen just under the $56 mark.

KRE: Breaks Out to a 2025 High

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on KRE. The valuation is attractive, while the technicals point to further upside. I am particularly encouraged by very bullish price action in what's usually a weak month.

data.title: The Coming Slowdown In Growth And Liquidity: What It Means For Investors In H2 2025
data.description:
By Raheel Siddiqui

Twin Perils: Slowing Economic Growth and Peaking Liquidity

Rapidly accelerating economic growth in the second quarter of 2025, combined with a massive first-half surge in global liquidity, fueled an against-all-odds rally in equity markets since their April trough. Now economic growth appears to be slowing and liquidity looks to be nearing a peak. In light of these consequential shifts, we believe lowering risk and decreasing leverage could be the most important adjustments investors should consider in the second half of 2025.

What is liquidity?

Liquidity refers to all the cash and near-cash circulating within financial markets and underpinning global credit creation.

Why does it matter?

Liquidity is a primary driver of market performance, especially for risk assets such as equities, high yield credit and cryptocurrencies. When liquidity is rising, equity valuations and interest in speculative assets increase; when liquidity is declining, investor appetite diminishes, borrowing costs increase and volatility begins to simmer.

Where is it headed?

While liquidity remains ample, we believe it could peak or plateau in the second half of 2025 as the number of global central banks cutting interest rates diminishes and the U.S. Treasury fortifies its cash balances.

What are the potential impacts?

A twin slowdown in economic growth and liquidity would likely result in higher volatility and weigh on various risk assets. As volatility rises, higher-quality stocks and defensive sectors may prove more resilient than speculative assets, including high-beta stocks, unprofitable tech, lower-quality small caps and cryptocurrencies, as well as cost-of-capital-sensitive assets such as real estate and high yield bonds.

Twin Drivers, Teflon Markets

GDP growth jumped from an annualized rate of -0.5% in 1Q to 3% in 2Q, while a $12 trillion surge in liquidity in the first half more than doubled the pace of liquidity expansion since Covid-19 pandemic. These twin drivers soothed the market’s concern about ongoing policy uncertainty, fueled a powerful equity rally, and led to extreme market distortions. Among other examples, in 2Q 2025, growth stocks outperformed value stocks by the second-largest margin since 1979; meanwhile, since the market bottom in early April, microcaps have outperformed every quadrant of the investment-style box, and retail investors' ownership of speculative assets (such as unprofitable technology companies) rose to a record level.

Recent GDP Growth And Surging Liquidity Have Increased Appetite For Risk

Growth stocks have outperformed value stocks

Microcaps have outpaced every style quadrant (Style Box Performance Apr. 8 to July 28)

Source: Neuberger Berman and FactSet, data as of July 28, 2025.

Past performance is not indicative of future results. Nothing herein constitutes a prediction or projection of future events or future market behavior. Due to a variety of factors, actual events or market behavior may differ significantly from any views expressed.

Twin Peak #1 - Slowing U.S. Growth

After a rebound in the second quarter, there is a growing consensus that U.S. economic growth will slow in the second half of the year. Goldman Sachs forecasts that U.S. GDP growth will slow from 3.0% (annualized) in 2Q 2025 to 1.0% in 2H 2025 (or perhaps even lower) driven by softening demand from government, corporations, manufacturers and consumers amid a potentially weakening labor market.

U.S. Economic Growth Is Expected To Slow In 2H 2025

Past performance is not indicative of future results. Nothing herein constitutes a prediction or projection of future events or future market behavior. Due to a variety of factors, actual events or market behavior may differ significantly from any views expressed. (Source: Goldman Sachs, Neuberger Berman and FactSet, data as of August 11, 2025.)

Twin Peak #1 - Slowing U.S. Growth (a Key Indicator)

We believe a dependable gauge of economic growth is the change in employment within sectors that tends to track the underlying economic cycle. At this juncture, we note deceleration in both cyclical and noncyclical employment. This suggests to us that weaker jobs data haven’t been entirely driven by DOGE cuts, and we expect a softening labor market will weigh on consumer spending going forward.

Softening Cyclical Employment (A Dependable Growth Gauge) Could Curb Consumer Spending

Twin Peak #2 - Liquidity (Fewer Central Banks Are Easing)

Easing monetary policy can spur economic activity (with a lag). As a slew of central banks began cutting interest rates in November 2022, the global PMI New Orders Index (a measure of global manufacturing activity) began to recover. Yet since October 2024, fewer central banks have eased policy—a potential sign, in our view, that global liquidity may have less central bank support going forward.

Fewer Central Banks Have Been Easing Policy, Which Could Pressure Liquidity

Twin Peak #2 - Liquidity (The U.S. Treasury Is Fortifying the TGA)

In addition to easing by fewer central banks, the U.S. Treasury is set to curb liquidity by fortifying the Treasury General Account (TGA). When the government draws on the TGA, it effectively injects cash into the economy. Between April 2024 and April 2025, the TGA fell by roughly two-thirds, to less than $300 billion; in July, officials said they plan to build it back up to $850 billion, effectively reducing overall liquidity in the system.

Treasury Officials Plan To Replenish The Primary U.S. Checking Account

Building up the Treasury General Account reduces overall liquidity in the system

The Links Between Market Liquidity and Risk Assets: Equity Returns

Excess liquidity makes its way to financial markets. The elevated correlation between market liquidity* and stock market returns in recent years suggests to us that liquidity is driving the market up and down more than it used to. Since 2022, the average correlation between market liquidity and the S&P 500 Index return has jumped from around 65% to north of 80%. We believe having a view on liquidity, therefore, is more important for investors today than it was in the past.

Market Liquidity Is Increasingly Driving Equity Returns

Having a view on liquidity may be more important today than it was in the past

The Links Between Market Liquidity and Risk Assets: Equity Valuations and High Yield Bond Spreads

We find that liquidity is highly correlated with both the valuation component of equity returns, as well as with high yield (HY) bond spreads. With equities, as liquidity increases, valuations tend to rise, and vice versa.

Since 2022, the correlation between market liquidity and the forward P/E ratio of the equally weighted S&P 500 Index is 72%.

With HY bonds, as market liquidity increases, bond spreads tend to compress. On the other hand, as liquidity contracts (and investors grow more risk-averse), bond spreads tend to widen, increasing financing costs and potentially curtailing future growth. Since 2022, the correlation between market liquidity and HY spreads is nearly -70%.

Liquidity Is Highly Correlated With Equity Valuations And HY Bond Spreads

As liquidity ebbs, equity valuations tend to contract while HY spreads tend to widen

The Links Between Economic Liquidity and Risk Assets: Money Supply

The correlation between economic liquidity and stock market returns has also risen in recent years. Unlike market liquidity, economic liquidity is measured by the money supply (M2): When global M2 rises, we find that risk assets tend to rise with it. Since 2023, the correlation between global M2 and the return on the Nasdaq 100 Index has been strong, recently hitting 87%. (We note the same correlation between M2 and the price of Bitcoin as well.)

As Money Supply Increases, Equities Tend To Rise

Plentiful Liquidity Increased the Market’s Appetite for Risk

As liquidity soared in the first half of 2025, equity investors embraced more risk, as shown by J.P. Morgan’s “High Beta Crowding” indicator. This proprietary metric captures the extent to which market returns accrue to the high beta factor (i.e., stocks with betas in the top decile of the overall market). In July, that crowding indicator recently reached the highest reading in 35 years. Extreme risk-driven distortions have historically signaled heightened vulnerability to reversals in equity markets and other risk assets.

Soaring Liquidity Has Sent Investors Flocking To High-Beta (i.e. Riskier) Assets

In July, J.P. Morgan’s proprietary “High Beta Crowding” indicator reached its highest peak in 35 years

What to Expect in a Twin Slowdown

As economic growth decelerates, risk-adjusted equity returns tend to shrink. Over the last three decades, we find that economic slowdowns tend to persist for 15 to 20 months, and that the S&P 500 Index tends to deliver meaningfully lower mid-single-digit annualized returns along with significantly higher volatility. If liquidity peaks as growth slows, we believe risk-adjusted equity returns could face further headwinds in the near to medium term and bring portfolio risk management once again into focus.

Decelerating Growth Tends To Shrink Equity Returns And Increase Volatility

If liquidity peaks as growth slows, risk-adjusted equity returns could face further headwinds

Tactical Considerations in a Twin Slowdown: Asset Classes

Over the last five years, the riskiest, unprofitable and most speculative assets have exhibited significant sensitivity to changes in economic liquidity (i.e. the money supply). If economic growth slows and liquidity peaks (as we expect), we believe investors would be better positioned by rotating out of riskier asset classes and into higher-quality, lower-beta, and more defensive equities, such as value stocks, equity-income names and high-quality small caps.

Riskier Asset Classes Tend To Be More Sensitive To Money Supply

As M2 peaks, value stocks, equity-income names and high-quality small caps may outperform 18%

Tactical Considerations in a Twin Slowdown: Sectors

If economic growth slows and liquidity peaks, we also believe investors would be better positioned by rotating out of higher-beta sectors, such as Technology and Consumer Discretionary, and into lower-beta, more defensive sectors, including Health Care, Utilities and Consumer Staples.

High-Beta Sectors Tend To Be More Sensitive To Money Supply

As M2 peaks, defensive sectors including Health Care, Utilities and Consumer Staples may outperform

Plotting a Course Through the Twin Peaks

While we believe liquidity remains ample for now, we caution investors to watch for signals of fading growth and liquidity in the second half of 2025. Here are some considerations we feel investors should keep in mind in a potential twin-peak slowdown:

Equities

As liquidity-driven markets fade, equity gains may depend more on earnings growth than multiple expansion, suggesting to us that stocks with lower valuations and stronger cash flows may prove more resilient.

Other Risk Assets

HY credit spreads could widen, raising borrowing costs and putting pressure on weaker companies. Additionally, real estate and other yield-driven assets may face headwinds as access to easy money further diminishes.

Portfolio Considerations

We believe the looming reversal of two meaningfully supportive market trends—rapid growth reacceleration and surging liquidity—could have significant implications for the second half of 2025. (If the U.S. dollar were to strengthen, it could tighten liquidity and quicken the arrival of the peak, while a weakening USD might delay it.) Considering these shifts, we recommend greater exposure to higher-quality, lower-beta, and moderately valued stocks and sectors, while reducing exposure to longer-duration, interest-rate-sensitive and higher-beta sectors. Furthermore, with volatility set to rise in 2H 2025, we also recommend reducing portfolio leverage and managing risk by focusing on corporate fundamentals, maintaining diversification and closely monitoring signals from central banks.

Index Definitions

The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of the large-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively higher price-to-book ratios, higher I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2-year) growth and higher sales per share historical growth (5 years).

The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2-year) growth and lower sales per share historical growth (5 years).

The MSCI ACWI Index captures large and mid-cap representation across 23 Developed Markets (DM) and 24 Emerging Markets (EM) countries. DM countries include: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the U.K. and the U.S. EM countries include: Brazil, Chile, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Egypt, Greece, Hungary, India, Indonesia, Korea, Kuwait, Malaysia, Mexico, Peru, Philippines, Poland, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey and United Arab Emirates. data.title: SHM: Fine As An Idea, Just Doesn't Work Out Well
data.description:
Grasping the details informs the whole

Or, to put that in less high falutin' language, if we understand how the bits of the machine work, then we can get to grips with how the machine as a whole works. This is as true of financial instruments as it is of any other construction we might want to try to understand.

With financial instruments, there's another level here too. There are various things that we might want to achieve through investing. Capital gains, long-term success, short-term speculation, income, all sorts of things. It's only by understanding how a specific financial instrument works, inside, that we can work out whether this is one that will aid in meeting those goals that we have.

This necessarily means a certain investigation of those internal details before we can reach the overall view.

SPDR Nuveen ICE Short Term Municipal Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:SHM)

This is an exchange-traded fund. This means that when we buy, our money goes into the fund - that, in turn, means that it will always trade fractions of a percentage point - and no more - off net asset value. It also means there's a theoretical risk of a redemption cascade. We all turn up asking for our money out at the same time, and investments have to be dumped to pay us. Whether that's actually a real risk or not depends upon the liquidity of the underlying investments. At least two commercial property funds have come to grief over this, for example. But here, investing in short-term municipal bonds, no, it's not a problem.

Bonds

We need a quick trot through bonds. These are loans to people but loans that can then be traded. Treasuries are lending to Uncle Sam, the Federal Government, and as they've not gone bust for a couple of centuries now, they're regarded as risk-free. This isn't wholly true - ask anyone who bought Continentals, or perhaps more usefully, those who bought certain Greek, or Argentine, government bonds. The US government hasn't gone bust, yes, but governments can and do. Still, we regard the Feds as being risk-free.

But this is credit-risk-free. There is still interest rate risk. This is the idea that yesterday interest rates were 3%. Today they're 4 - just as an example you understand. A bond issued yesterday pays $3 per $100 for the life of the bond and one today $4. The two bonds are obviously worth different amounts - the difference of $1 per year per $100 for the life of the bond. The price difference is greater the longer the life—the "tenor"- of the bond. That value difference is the net present value of that $ a year times the tenor of the bond. 30 year bonds change value more than 30 day ones, obviously.

This then interacts with a grand truth about bond - or even loan - markets. We get a higher coupon, a higher interest rate, the longer the loan/bond. We have to accept that greater interest rate risk in order to gain the higher, and longer-term, interest rate.

This specific fund gives us an interesting example of this:

Interest rates have been declining recently. This fund is in fairly short-term bonds. So, the bonds that it bought a couple of years ago have a higher capital value than par - they were issued at higher than current interest rates. But this also means that as they come to maturity and are redeemed at par, their capital value declines to par. That's why the coupon rate on the holdings is above the actual return on the fund.

The second type of bond is corporate, and they can go bust. So, they pay a higher interest rate/coupon but at the expense of credit risk. The way to deal with credit risk is to be diversified, and thus, if there's one failure, that's annoying but not a disaster. It's difficult for us as private investors to be diversified in bonds - especially ones already in issue. We can buy at issue in $1,000 lots, normally, but the secondary markets can, like the European corporate ones, deal in minimum lot sizes of $200k par value. That's probably beyond us as individuals, to be able to hold many positions at that size. So, funds, the fund owns many positions, we own some tiny fraction of the fund.

The third type is municipals - lending to subnational government but still official bodies, from the state to the local water board. There is interest rate risk here, as well as credit risk (but the default rate is some 0.1% by one estimate, so very small), so munis should pay some interest rate between Treasuries and corporates. But they don't - they pay less than Treasuries.

Tax equivalent yield

The reason munis pays a lower coupon is because the income is free of Federal income tax (and for instate bonds, usually of state income tax to instate holders). What we're all interested in, obviously, about any investment is what do we end up with, cash, after we've paid our taxes? So, if we've not got to pay tax, then that "real" yield is higher. But by how much? Well, that depends upon our own marginal - the rate upon the next $ of income - tax rate. Which makes it difficult to compare.

So, what we do is work out what a taxable investment would have to pay to produce the same final, cash in hand, income as this untaxed one? The tax equivalent yield. Not a wholly easy calculation, but fortunately people publish tables of this:

We can now compare a theoretical investment in taxable Treasuries, or corporates, against munis.

Interim conclusions

Longer bonds pay higher than shorter, greater credit risk more than lower, Treasuries and corporates are taxable income, munis are - and we can ignore, here, the exceptions when they are not - non-taxable income. So we've a certain mix and match of possibilities here at maximising our income while reducing our risk - this being the grand issue in financial markets. Yes, of course, we have to take risk. We also get paid for taking risk. Finally the grand mistake in a financial market is to be taking risk and not being paid for it.

This specific fund, SHM

As we've already seen, this is in short-term municipals. So, low credit risk and also low interest rate risk. Not none of either, but low.

The SPDR® Nuveen ICE Short Term Municipal Bond ETF seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the price and yield performance of the ICE 1‑5 Year AMT‑Free Broad Municipal Index

They're trying to track an index. Now, I have an opinion about this. They're holding near 900 positions out of a possible universe of near 15,000, as they say. So, they are being selective. But my opinion - and please do note I'm aware that opinions can be wrong - is that tracking indices in the muni market doesn't work in the sense of delivering goodly amounts of cash into our wallets. Index, or index following, funds like VTES and VTEC, just don't produce good incomes - I claim because this is an opinion. Being much more selective does produce returns in this market - again a claim I'm making.

The full holdings can be seen here.

SHM is also sticking with the good credit risks:

Again, I have an opinion on this. I'm not wholly sure - note how opinion gets expressed as a maybe? - that worrying about muni credit ratings is really a sensible thing. The overall default rate is so low that, well, as I've said before:

Now "not rated" in the bond markets usually means something like "complete dog and not touching it with your bargepole let alone mine". CCC isn't much better. Actually, in large parts of the bond market, BBB isn't thought of as much better than that.

At which point we could just assume that SHYM is achieving high yield by taking on lots of credit risk—the standard complaint is that we can only get paid in financial markets by taking risk.

However, the actual default rate in the muni markets is really very low—that 0.1% estimate above. So we could be dealing with comparative credit worthiness, not actual. Sure, a BBB muni is less creditworthy than an AAA but if the overall default rate is tiny, how much difference does that actually make?

Also, not rating means that the issuer hasn't paid a rating agency to rate the bond. This is not uncommon for small issues either. Fund managers can still decide upon their own opinion by having their own look at the finances of the issuer. That is, "unrated" does not mean "not worthy of a rating" it means "no one has paid for a rating" which is a very different thing.

Effectively, a fund heavily into unrated bonds is using the calculations of its own managers rather than the calculations of the rating agencies. Many funds won't touch unrated bonds, so there's a possibility that they're underpriced. Maybe, possibly, we cannot be categorical about this.

That, I think, is the special secret sauce here. Grasping that unrated in the muni market does not mean high credit risk, even if unrated (and other low-rating bonds) pay higher coupons. Thus, without a (substantial that is) increase in risk, it is possible to gain a higher income. Which is the great trick in any financial market—to get paid for taking risks you're not actually taking or to be overpaid for the ones you are.

And that, I think, is where the problem is here.

The expense ratio, at 0.2%, well, that's high for merely holding an index, actually fairly low for proper active management, but then that's what the fund is doing. Not holding the universe of the index but a large enough portion of it to track that index.

The big issue

For me the big issue is this:

Yield (TTM) 2.46%

That's the Seeking Alpha measure. Or if we prefer, this:

That's not, to my mind, good enough. That's only fractionally ahead of inflation which means that the real income here - after re-investing enough to maintain capital position - is, well, it's not much, is it?

Why I'm wrong

There could be some reason why a low income is preferable for some investors. I can't think of why that might be, but it is possible. We're in short-term bonds here, so a capital gain is unlikely on the face of it. But perhaps there is some reason why investors would like this. I would suggest that we shouldn't like this, but then of course I can be wrong about other peoples' preferences.

My opinion

I'm wholly fine with the idea that people would like to be in relatively short-term bonds in order to reduce interest rate risk. Munis for the tax benefits. Munis and/or Treasuries for the low credit risks. Sure, all of this makes great sense as a strategy. I'm just left with the feeling that this particular implementation of it doesn't quite measure up. We can get near two whole percentage points better from SHYM. Which does follow largely the same investing strategy, even if with a little twist to it.

I don't think there's anything wrong with SHM. I just think we can do better. So, of course, we should go and do better.

data.title: NNN REIT: A Dividend Aristocrat In Net Lease With Hidden Upside
data.description:
Thesis

NNN REIT (NNN) seems to combine the best of both worlds: a rare reliability in dividend distribution and, at the same time, growth prospects arising from targeted acquisitions and a strong capital structure.

In my own assessment, the company is currently at a stage where the investor can lock in an attractive dividend yield of around 5.6%, without worrying much about financial instability or increased operational risk.

The secret lies in how management handles the cycle, selling weak properties, recycling capital into new investments with strong tenants and long-term contracts, while strengthening its liquidity through careful debt issuances.

All this creates a solid base for continued AFFO and dividend growth, with potential outperformance compared to the sector.

The stock deserves a buy recommendation, which fits very defensive equity portfolios.

Stock Overview

The stock of NNN has gained steady investor trust thanks to its long-term record of consistency.

From the point of view of stock performance, NNN does not impress with explosive rallies, but rewards steadily through dividend.

The stock is obviously very defensive, something that is not necessarily bad. On the contrary, it fits exactly a likewise defensive investor, who seeks steady, very secured dividends (as we will see also below) from a very high-quality company, without worrying much about the fluctuations in the stock price.

Comparing with other very good companies in the sector, such as Regency Centers (REG), Agree (ADC), Getty Realty (GTY), Realty Income (O) and Brixmor Property Group (BRX), we can see that it stands decently, neither impressing nor disappointing.

In any case, the “moderate” return it offers comes along with the high degree of safety that things will continue smoothly. Something that interests those long-term investors who want to be positioned for years, enjoying a safe dividend.

5.6% Yield With Room To Grow

In my opinion, the strongest card of NNN is the dividend. And it is not only about the 5.6% yield, which is already attractive. What matters is the consistency, 36 consecutive years of increases. In a REIT universe where many struggle to keep distributions stable, NNN manages to build year by year greater trust.

The recent dividend increase by 3.4% (to $0,60 per quarter) signals that management sees the AFFO sufficiently covered. With payout ratio at 71% of AFFO, there is a significant safety margin. For the second quarter, the free cash flow after the distribution was about $50 million, offering room for reinvestment or future increases.

What convinces me more is that the dividend increases are not based on occasional profits, but on a steady growth of AFFO. The guidance for 2025 was raised to $3,40, $3,45, compared to the previous estimate of $3,39, something that shows consistency in the goal of long-term growth of 1% to 2% per year.

High is also the quality of cash flow, even in weaker markets, the reductions were limited, a fact that strengthens the image of a “sleep well at night” REIT.

In the second quarter of 2025, the company announced core FFO $0,84 per share and AFFO $0.85, increased by 1.2% on an annual basis.

The annual base rent [ABR] reached $894 million, increased by almost 7% compared to last year, while the net operating income margin (NOI margin) was at 98%, levels that show excellent operational control, while it presents better alignment of revenues and expenses and higher stability of return.

Recycling Capital Into 17-Year Leases

NNN operates with an unexciting yet productive business model. The company focuses on buying single-tenant properties, which generate 7.4% initial returns and maintain leases for more than 17 years on average.

The company continues its investments in Q2 2025 by buying 45 new properties worth $230 million. Additionally, spent $460 million on total acquisitions during the semester. This shows that the company remains active through strategic partnership deals, which resulted in 8 out of 11 transactions with existing partners, thus minimizing potential deal failures.

The management team actively removes properties from their portfolio when they fail to show promising growth potential. The second quarter brought $50 million from selling 23 properties, while the half-year sales reached $65 million from 33 properties. The company demonstrates effective value creation through capital recycling because it sells properties at prices 170 basis points below its new investment yields.

The lease renewal rate reached 85% with rent increases at 8% above previous levels, and new leases provided 5% rent growth. The retail portfolio demonstrates strong market performance because it withstands the current retail market challenges. The “At Home” bankruptcy serves as an example to prove the rule because NNN owns strategically located properties that attract new tenants when existing leases expire.

$1.5B Liquidity, 11-Year Debt, Zero Surprises

The capital structure of NNN is among the safest in the sector. With average debt maturity of 11 years and no exposure to floating or secured borrowing, the company enjoys rare stability. The $500 million 5-year bond with a coupon of 4.6%, which was issued recently, strengthened liquidity, which reaches $1,5 billion.

In my opinion, the key element is that the company can fully finance this year’s acquisitions ($600-$700 million) without depending on external capital under pressure. Leverage is around 5,7x, slightly increased compared to the first quarter, but expected due to advanced acquisitions. The CFO has set a target of returning below 5,5x, something that I consider realistic, given the strong free cash flow.

In addition, the company took advantage of the rise of the stock to issue 254,000 new shares through ATM, raising $11 million. This selective use of equity shows healthy management of cost of capital and not dependence on continuous dilution.

Risks: Tenant Failures, Tariff Shocks & The Battle For Yield

There are factors that could create challenges. The macroeconomic environment is one factor. Higher interest rates don’t directly hurt the company because of its long-term debt, but they make the competition tougher. This leads to compressed cap rates, especially in large portfolios, reducing the ability of the Company to invest comfortably. I consider that the upcoming decrease of interest rates from the Fed will not create particular problems for the Company. On the contrary there may be buying interest. NNN attracts defensive portfolios, that may want to take slightly higher risk than other kinds of safe investments, such as bonds, for correspondingly slightly higher return. Meaning that these investors will prefer companies, like NNN, when the bond yields will show a decrease.

Then, there is the tenant risk. Although the portfolio is diversified with more than 400 tenants, the recent bankruptcy of "At Home" shows that retail chains are not invulnerable. Even though NNN has good property positioning, a series of bankruptcies could pressure AFFO, even temporarily. The restaurant sector and furniture, consumer goods are more vulnerable to recession or to increased tariffs.

A third element is the portfolio recycling strategy. Although until today it has proved profitable, it depends on the company always finding buyers at good valuations. If liquidity in the sector decreases, NNN may struggle to dispose of weak properties without losses.

Finally, political and tax factors, such as changes in digital taxes or tariff measures, can hurt tenants and indirectly increase risk. The company itself recognizes it, incorporating 60 bps of bad debt in the forecasts for 2025. Although until now only 15% has been used, these risks are not negligible.

The risks are manageable, but investors should be aware that the stock is not fully protected against recession or massive pressure in retail.

Conclusion

In my opinion, NNN is a stock that deserves to be in every portfolio that seeks a combination of steady income and predictable growth.

With dividend yield of 5.6% (record of 36 consecutive increases), strong capital structure, and a careful buy and sell strategy, it offers reassurance in an environment full of uncertainty.

Despite the risks related to tenants and the broader economic framework, management shows that it knows how to protect the value of the portfolio.

With the guidance for AFFO increasing and the balance sheet remaining strong, I consider that the stock maintains upward momentum.

data.title: Nvidia: Data Center Growth Slowdown Is Expected To Continue Into CY2026
data.description:
Buyside Consensus Was Higher

NVIDIA Corporation's (NASDAQ:NVDA) (TSX:NVDA:CA) has continued to rally since its April low and has become one of the most important market indicators. Why? Because the equity market is largely driven by AI-related growth, which has pushed valuations higher. NVDA remains the best-in-class AI play. I believe that we are in a totally different market cycle. The GenAI boom is unique compared to previous economic cycles. AI growth momentum can significantly lift market sentiment and overshadow S&P 500 names outside of the Magnificent 7. The stronger the AI-related growth, the higher the market consensus, but the key question is: when will demand eventually slow?

The stock got hit with an initial 4% pullback following 2Q FY2026 earnings, but those losses were mostly recouped the next trading day. 2Q Data Center revenue missed market expectations, and management signaled that H20 hasn't contributed to revenue in China.

In my last coverage, I downgraded the stock from Buy to Hold, citing the risk of the stock rising too far, too fast. However, a neutral rating doesn't mean the stock has no long-term value. It means that chasing the rally at the current levels could face higher downside risks. Timing the market is difficult, and investors seem increasingly comfortable with high valuations supported by strong AI demand. Given the ongoing growth deceleration, I believe upside earnings surprises could narrow. Moreover, signs of an AI demand slowdown in 2026 may create cyclical headwinds for NVDA. Therefore, I'm cautious on the recent rally and reiterating a Hold rating on the stock.

2Q FY2026 Earnings Takeaway

NVDA delivered strong 2Q FY2026 earnings, beating both revenue and non-GAAP EPS consensus, driven by strong demand for Blackwell chips (GB200 and GB300). However, growth expectations were already priced in, particularly around Data Center revenue, which grew 56.4% YoY growth but fell short of buyside consensus. As shown on the chart, growth momentum still remains impressive but is starting to normalize.

Despite H20 headwinds in 2Q, the company still achieved a 5% sequential growth in Data Center. During the earnings call, Jensen Hwang noted that H20 revenue declined by $4 billion due to missed China sales under licensing restrictions. However, NVDA released nearly $180 million of previously reserved H20 inventory, helping lift the growth margin to 72.7%.

While the U.S. has lifted the H20 ban for some Chinese customers and Jensen even said that "bringing Blackwell to China is a real possibility", I think uncertainties remain high. The PBOC recently expressed concerns over information risks and urged Chinese firms to avoid H20 chips. Even if they adopt them, competition from domestic AI alternatives such as Huawei is intensifying. On top of that, the U.S. government has also signaled that they want a 15% cut of H20 revenue from China sales. That said, political risk for NVDA in China remains high, at least for now.

3Q FY2026 Guidance: Growth Slowdown Continues, Margins Improving

Looking ahead, its 3Q FY2026 guidance signals that continued strong AI demand. The midpoint of 3Q revenue guidance implies 53.9% YoY growth, showing a sequential slowdown. However, management noted that this outlook excludes H20 revenue from China. If licensing issues resolve, the company expects an additional $2 billion to $5 billion in revenue, which could lift to 64% YoY (assuming $3.5 billion H20 revenue from China), indicating a sequential rebound.

Gross margin is expected to recover to 73.5% in 3Q. Management had previously guided for gross margins in the mid-70s by late FY2026, suggesting 4Q FY2026 gross margin could increase another 100-150 bps QoQ. Over the longer term, Jensen reiterated a $3-4 trillion AI infrastructure TAM by 2030. Overall, management sees near-term demand remains extraordinarily strong, but growth momentum is gradually trending lower, as shown on the chart.

Potential Slowdown in AI Spending in CY2026?

The stock's impact on whether AI spending continues to be robust or softens will depend on how much is already priced in. According to a recent report, overall data center spend is expected to grow 42% YoY in 2025, accelerating from 40.3% YoY in 2024. This has fueled robust ongoing AI demand. However, we saw NVDA's revenue growth is slowing due to the law of large numbers.

Gartner projects a sharp slowdown in 2026, with data center spending growth dropping to 16.3%. Meanwhile, despite some de-escalation in tariffs, Trump's trade war is not over. If tariff risk increases, the uncertainty could lead companies to pause new AI projects, as the report indicated.

Recently, Trump has threatened to impose new tariffs and export controls on advanced technology and semiconductors in response to digital services taxes and has also floated a potential 200% tariff on China over a rare-earth dispute. It seems the market has shrugged off these risks and decided not to react until they actually materialize.

The key question is whether AI demand can once again surpass market expectations and whether the next-gen AI platform, Rubin, can sustain growth momentum. Rubin is scheduled for volume production in 2026.

Can Valuation Expand Further?

The short answer is yes. But my neutral view reflects the fact that growth is unlikely to be linear. NVDA has rallied more than 12 folds in 5 years, reflecting its best-in-class growth profile but also banking on lofty AI expectations. Despite long-term secular AI tailwinds, I believe the data center business will eventually revert to a more cyclical pattern, which can cause a stock pullback.

At 41x non-GAAP P/E, NVDA doesn't look too expensive given the high valuation of equity market. However, regulatory headwinds and intensifying Chinese competition could reshape long-term growth expectations. Since the relentless rally implies that the company must deliver consistently extraordinary results going forward, I believe the risk of missing expectations is higher, particularly as it will need to explore new market opportunities to sustain long-term growth. However, I think it's fine to hold existing positions, but it seems imprudent to chase the rally at current levels.

Conclusion

In the near term, NVDA's stock momentum could continue, as its 2Q FY2026 earnings demonstrated resiliency and management signaled no slowdown in AI demand. In the mid to long term, however, the miss in Data Center revenue and slowing growth, political risks, and potential demand for H20 chips should be cautious. The stock's valuation has nearly priced in perfection, which is reasonable given the current AI demand. After a straight-line rally, the risk of the near-term consolidation is higher, and the upside potential is limited. I see more attractive buying opportunities around the $150 level and remain Hold on NVDA for now.

data.title: CrowdStrike: Valuation Potential Fully Realized
data.description:
Cybersecurity firm CrowdStrike (CRWD) reported better-than-expected earnings for its second fiscal quarter on Wednesday due to strong growth in its customer pool and expansion in its annual recurring revenue base. However, CrowdStrike’s guidance for the third quarter disappointed investors, leading to a 4% drop of the company’s shares in the after-hours market.

CrowdStrike remained widely profitable on a free cash flow basis and continues to grow its ARR business at 20% annual growth rates, with an acceleration expected in the second half of the year. While the ARR performance was very strong, and the outlook for Q3 was not bad, I believe CrowdStrike has very limited upside revaluation potential. In my opinion, the cybersecurity platform's ARR and earnings growth potential is more than fully reflected in the company's valuation, and I maintain a rating of sell at the current valuation point.

Previous rating

I downgraded shares of CrowdStrike in May 2025 due to concerns about valuation: Time To Take Profits. Since then shares have dropped about 8%, not including the 4% after-hours drop on Wednesday. I like that CrowdStrike is making gains in terms of its ARR business and that the cybersecurity platform is heavily involved in growing its AI offers. However, given the stretched valuation based off of revenue, I believe the upside potential is likely limited here.

CrowdStrike beat Q2 '26 estimates

The cybersecurity enterprise reported better-than-expected results on both the top and bottom lines for the second fiscal quarter: CrowdStrike generated $0.93 in adjusted earnings per share in the June quarter, which beat the consensus estimate by $0.10 per share. Revenues came in at $1.17B, beating the average prediction by $20M.

The second quarter was a good one for CrowdStrike, which benefited from continual customer acquisition in the enterprise market and increasing annual recurring revenues. The cybersecurity platform generated $1,169M in revenue in Q2 '26, showing 21% year-over-year growth. The important metric of 'subscription revenue' came in at $1,103M, also showing 20% year-over-year growth. Subscription revenue, which forms the basis for CrowdStrike's growing ARR, represented 95% of the platform's total revenue in the June quarter.

What stood out from CrowdStrike’s Q2 '26 earnings scorecard was that the company continued to make considerable progress in building its ARR business. ARR stands for annual recurring revenue and is the result of subscription solutions for which customers pay on a recurring basis.

CrowdStrike's ARR stood at $4,657M at the end of Q2 '26, meaning the cybersecurity enterprise added $792M in annual recurring revenue in the trailing-twelve-month period, which calculates to about $200M per quarter. The company's ARR is growing at 20% annually, but the firm has guided for at least 40% net new ARR growth in the second half of the year as enterprise customers adopt the firm's Cloud and AI-driven products and services.

At the current rate of growth, the cybersecurity platform is on track to reach at least $5.0B in ARR by the end of the current fiscal year. A sustained 20% expansion rate in annual recurring revenue in the next five years could put the company on track to $10.0B in ARR by FY 2030.

In terms of free cash flow, CrowdStrike also had a decent second fiscal quarter: it generated $284M in FCF, showing 4% year-over-year growth. The free cash flow margin was 24%, down 4 PP Y/Y. While the free cash flow margin dropped year-over-year, CrowdStrike has been consistently profitable in the last twelve months and should remain so going forward.

Outlook Q3 '26 disappointed

CrowdStrike guided for $1,208-1,218M in revenue for the third quarter, which was below the consensus estimate of $1,230M. While the guidance for Q3 '26 fell short of the estimate, the company raised its full-year top line outlook at the low-end by $6M (to a new guidance low point of $4,749.5M) while its EPS outlook was raised from $3.44-3.56/share to $3.60-3.72/share. The slightly disappointing Q3 '26 outlook likely explains why shares of the cybersecurity platform dropped 4% in the after-hours market.

CrowdStrike’s earnings growth potential is likely overvalued

In my last work on the cybersecurity platform, I stated that I was concerned about the company’s high valuation based off of forward revenue, which is why I downgraded CrowdStrike to sell at the time. Shares of CrowdStrike are currently valued at a FY 2026 revenue multiplier of 18.0X, which implies that the cybersecurity business is trading approximately 30% above its 3-year average price-to-revenue ratio of 13.9X. The high valuation relative to the historical average is an indication that CrowdStrike is still overvalued: if shares of CrowdStrike were to revalue to the historical P/S average of 13.9X, they could have a fair value closer to $326/share, implying about 23% downside correction potential.

Other cloud-based cybersecurity platforms that offer Cloud, Network and Endpoint security include Palo Alto (PANW), SentinelOne (S) and Zscaler (ZS) which I am using in my industry group below for comparison purposes. CrowdStrike is still the most expensive cybersecurity play here for investors and is currently priced at a 63% premium to the industry group average price-to-revenue ratio of only 11.1X.

Risks with CrowdStrike

The biggest risk for CrowdStrike is a slowdown in the ARR business, which is essential to backing up the cybersecurity company’s valuation. Strong ARR growth (20%+ annually), however, seems to me to be already priced into the firm's valuation, so a slowdown in annual recurring revenue expansion is likely the biggest risk factor for the company right now. What would get me to change my mind about the platform is the use of FCF for new revenue-boosting acquisitions or an acceleration in the firm's ARR growth trajectory.

Final thoughts

Although CrowdStrike submitted a very solid earnings sheet for the second quarter with 20%+ Y/Y growth in revenue, strong FCF, and a double beat on earnings and revenue, I stand by my previous conclusion that the cybersecurity platform is likely overvalued, based off of revenue, and therefore has an unattractive risk profile for investors. I see long-term ARR growth potential for the platform in an environment where the cyberthreat landscape is constantly evolving. However, I believe that CrowdStrike's long-term ARR and revenue growth potential in the cybersecurity market is more than fully reflected in the company’s valuation at this point... which is why I confirm my sell rating for CrowdStrike following the Q2 '26 earnings report.

data.title: Risk Managing An Evergreen Private Credit Portfolio
data.description:
By Mark Garfinkel

As the private credit market surges toward $3 trillion in assets1, concerns are growing over the potential formation of a bubble—driven by too much capital chasing too few high-profile lending opportunities. Rather than focusing on whether the market is in a bubble, this article explores how to strategically manage risk within a private credit portfolio.

Key Highlights:

  • Capital Concentration in Large Loans: Most capital inflows have targeted larger, private equity-sponsored businesses with EBITDA over $100M. This space is now highly efficient and saturated, characterized by tighter spreads, commoditized loans, and weakening loan terms—challenging risk-adjusted return potential.
  • Potential Alpha in Inefficient Markets: Risk can be better managed by investing in less competitive, undercapitalized niches such as smaller borrowers, specialty lending, and asset-based finance. These areas can offer higher yields, better loan terms, and lower loan-to-value ratios due to reduced competition.
  • Three Pillars of Risk Management:
    1. Diversification across segments, strategies, and issuers to mitigate volatility and avoid concentration risk.
    2. Credit Management through stringent underwriting, partner selection, and disciplined loan structuring.
    3. Liquidity Management using short-duration, laddered investments and cash-equivalent buffers to preserve flexibility and access.
  • Not All Private Credit Is Created Equal: Like equities, the private credit market is heterogeneous. Opportunities vary dramatically by market segment, and risk-adjusted return potential is highest in those niches that are overlooked and underfunded.

Ultimately, prudent portfolio construction and active risk management—especially outside of crowded upper-market segments—are the keys to the opportunity for enhanced risk-adjusted returns from private credit investing in 2025 and beyond.

Risk Managing a Private Credit Portfolio in a Crowded Market

As private credit grows into a $3 trillion asset class, concerns over risk and overvaluation loom large. Yet, the solution isn’t avoiding private credit—it’s about managing it wisely.

Private credit refers to loans made by non-bank lenders directly to private businesses, a segment that expanded rapidly post-2008 as regulatory constraints limited bank lending. Today, the market faces a new challenge: excess capital flowing into a narrow segment of large, private equity-sponsored companies.

Private Credit Explosion Across Debt Types

This chart illustrating how combined credit categories—including distressed, venture, real estate, BDCs, and infrastructure debt—have surged more than tenfold post‑GFC.

Where the Capital is Going

Most inflows have chased large borrowers—companies with EBITDA above $100 million and loan needs well into the hundreds of millions. This influx has commoditized that part of the market. With widespread competition, interest spreads have compressed, covenants have loosened, and lenders have taken on more risk to win deals.

This chart demonstrates that most private credit deal flow continues to favor larger borrowers (>$100M), with the highest capital concentration in the mid to upper end of the market. Notable takeaways:

  • Borrowers under $100M remain underrepresented in terms of volume, even as total market deal flow grows.
  • From 2015 to 2023, deal volume peaked around 2022, with notable share growth in $1B+ deals

In this highly efficient environment, outsized returns become harder to find. But does that mean all private credit is overvalued? Not at all.

Value Flows from the Forgotten Streams

The largest areas of the private lending market, much like the largest river systems, have been fully explored and mapped. These are the obvious, well-traveled channels of capital that are visible, deep, and widely pursued by institutional investors because it is familiar, liquid, and scalable. Yet there are thousands, if not millions, of smaller tributaries ripe for further exploration. While these areas are not always as obvious to the naked eye, proper exploration can identify undercapitalized and/or misunderstood lending niches that flow with potential. The overlooked corners of the private credit world—small businesses, niche lenders, and specialized asset-based finance—offer higher yields, tighter covenants, and better collateralization. These borrowers are often too small for large private credit firms and constrained by traditional bank lending standards.

This conceptual graphic is designed to illustrate:

  • Heavy capital chasing large PE-sponsored loans (EBITDA > $100M, commoditized, covenant-light)
  • Smaller, underserved lending niches with stronger protections and yield

For risk-conscious investors, this is fertile ground. Markets with fewer lenders and less capital create favorable dynamics: lenders can command better terms, reduce loan-to-value, and maintain underwriting discipline. These inefficiencies enable enhanced risk-adjusted returns without taking undue risk.

Risk Management Framework

The most effective way to manage risk in private credit is through a three-pronged strategy:

  1. Diversification
    • Invest across multiple segments (e.g., real estate, legal finance, factoring, consumer finance).
    • Maintain limits on exposure per strategy or borrower.
    • Adjust position sizing to reduce downside risk in the event of a loss in any one segment.
  2. Credit Management
    • Partner with lending specialists who focus on niche markets and have proven underwriting capabilities.
    • Look for strong collateral structures, tight loan terms, and stringent covenant enforcement.
    • Emphasize capital preservation over yield chasing.
  3. Liquidity and Duration Management
    • Incorporate short-term private investments and liquid public assets to maintain flexibility.
    • Ladder maturities to provide periodic reinvestment opportunities.
    • Keep overall portfolio duration short to adapt quickly to changing market conditions.

Private Credit is not Monolithic

The phrase "private credit" encompasses a vast and varied universe. Treating it as a single asset class ignores the crucial differences across market segments. Just as one wouldn’t evaluate all equities through the lens of tech stocks, one shouldn’t judge private credit solely by the dynamics of large direct lending deals.

There’s opportunity in specialization—and risk in generalization.

Final Thought

Private credit remains a compelling tool for generating income and diversification. However, sustainable success requires strategic allocation, focused underwriting, and active management. In a market increasingly dominated by large players chasing the same deals, the real potential alpha lies off the beaten path.

In private credit, it pays to be selective. The optimal fishing holes are often the quietest ones.
