data.title: Netflix: Shares Are Getting Perilously Close To Warranting A Downgrade
data.description:
One business that continues to surprise me is streaming giant Netflix (NFLX). Back in April of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘hold’ candidate. After management reported financial results for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year that exceeded analysts’ expectations, I called it a ‘great show’ that was happening. Growth was continuing and the future looked bright. I don't mind paying a high price for a high-quality company, especially if it operates as an industry leader. But because of how expensive the stock was, I had no choice but to reaffirm it as a ‘hold’ candidate at that time.

Since then, the company has performed pretty much in line with what I anticipated. You see, whenever I rate a company a ‘hold’, I am making the claim that shares are likely to perform pretty much along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future. Since that article was published, the stock is up 24.5%. That comes on the back of an upward revision in revenue and profits for this year. However, it also almost perfectly matches the 24% increase enjoyed by the broader market. From a valuation standpoint, I must say that things are getting a bit extreme. I'm not ready to downgrade the company just yet. But it really wouldn't take much more for that to be justified. For now, however, I will keep it as a very cautious 'hold.'

A growth machine

The most recent data that we have regarding Netflix covers through the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. For that time, revenue for the company came in at $11.08 billion. That represents an increase of 15.9% compared to the $9.56 billion the company reported one year earlier. In the past, management used to provide region by region subscriber numbers and monthly ARPU (average revenue per user). To this day, I still think it was a disservice to investors for management to discontinue that. According to management, this move was the result of the company continuing to expand its business model, including in advertising in ways that made a reliance on user-specific data less relevant. But I digress.

According to management, this increase in sales came as a result of more members spread across all regions, higher subscription pricing, and continued growth in advertising revenue. In some areas, like its UCAN (US and Canada) region, revenue growth was 15% year over year.

That in particular was thanks to price changes that the company implemented previously. The business continues to make great progress when it comes to monetizing its world-class platform. As an example, the firm expects to roughly double its advertising revenue this year compared to last year. This follows the rollout of its Netflix Ads Suite, which is its own in-house first-party ad technology platform. The second quarter of 2025 was the first quarter in which this operation had been available to all of its ad markets. Management even described their ad tech platform as being ‘foundational’ to its long-term ads strategy.

On top of this, the business was almost complete at the time that it announced results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, with closing most of its deals with major ad agencies that will allow it to approximately double ads revenue in the US alone for this year.

The company continues to benefit from other things as well. As an example, during the second quarter of this year, management redesigned its TV homepage in a way that they believe will improve member experience. Supposedly, early traction on that has been encouraging. In fact, management even said that it has exceeded expectations.

Of course, no conversation can be had without talking about the core operations of the business. This includes strong performance associated with much of the content on its platform. As an example, during the quarter, Sirens experienced 56 million views. Ginny & Georgia S3 raked in 53 million. New series that are out in the US, such as The Four Seasons and Ransom Canyon, brought in 40 million views and 30 million views, respectively. And some films that the company released did incredibly well.

As an example of this, Extraterritorial which is from Germany, became its fourth most popular non-English language film in the firm's history with 89 million views. But even that does not match the 109 million views that Tyler Perry’s STRAW received. But perhaps the most impressive showing ended up being attributed to Squid Game S3, with a whopping 122 million views. Naturally, management is continuing to focus on upcoming releases as well. Even though a date has not yet been set, the one that I am the most excited for is season 2 of the One Piece live action series. That should come out sometime next year.

Speaking of One Piece, one thing that I need to bring up from a previous article about a different company is the firms position in the anime industry. On August 14th of this year, I published an article about how Webtoon Entertainment Inc. (WBTN) and The Walt Disney Company (DIS) had reached an agreement to partner up in order to bring Disney content, including original Disney content, to its platform. In that article, I pointed out how Netflix had only recently said that half of its global users now watch anime, with anime viewership tripling on the platform over the last five years. The Japanese anime industry is worth around $23.2 billion. And if everything goes according to plan, this industry will see significant growth between now and the end of this decade. As one of the big players in the anime industry, the other one being Crunchyroll, which is owned by Sony (SONY), I fully expect continued growth in the years to come.

The growth that the company saw on its top line allowed bottom line results to improve as well. Net income jumped from $2.15 billion to $3.13 billion year over year. Operating cash flow expanded from $1.29 billion to $2.42 billion. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an approximate doubling from $1.54 billion to $3.11 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the streaming giant grew from $2.68 billion to $3.85 billion. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, are all growing materially year over year.

Things are going so well for Netflix right now that management even decided to increase guidance for this year. They now expect revenue to be between $44.8 billion and $45.2 billion. That compares to the prior expected range of between $43.5 billion and $44.5 billion. For context, last year, the company generated sales of just $39 billion. So if management hits the midpoint of guidance this year, that would be a 15.4% rise in overall sales. On top of this, management expects a small increase in operating margin. Instead of the 29% margin that they expect to previously, they now expect that to be 29.5%. I know this might not sound like much of a difference. But when applied to $45 billion in revenue, that's an extra $225 million in operating income compared to what the picture would be without the upward revision.

If we project out results based on what we have seen so far for the year, we would anticipate net profits of $11.70 billion, adjusted operating cash flow of $12.15 billion, and EBITDA of $14.48 billion. If we use these estimates, and we also use the historical results that Netflix achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. Honestly, these are really high multiples. They would normally be high enough for me to easily rate the company a ‘sell’.

But at the same time, this is a quality company that's growing rather rapidly. The firm has only a small amount of debt, $6.06 billion, when stacked up against a market capitalization of $519.90 billion. And we need to consider the stickiness that the platform has experienced in recent years, it's difficult to be bearish.

Takeaway

Right now, I do think that Netflix is a fascinating business. But honestly, it's too expensive for my liking. It's not so expensive that I would downgrade the company just yet. But it is getting there. For now, however, I will be content with maintaining a ‘hold’ rating on the stock.
